title: Lecture 5: Conjoint Analysis for Product Design

cards.definition: The assumption that a product's total utility is the sum of the part-worth utilities of its individual attribute levels, with no cross-attribute interaction effects.
An important product characteristic that drives consumer choice in conjoint analysis. Each attribute has two or more levels.
The reference level of an attribute omitted from OLS regression and against which all other levels are compared. Its utility is set to zero by convention and captured in the intercept.
A conjoint variant in which respondents choose their most preferred option from a set of competing profiles rather than rating or ranking. More closely mirrors real purchasing behavior.
A survey-based statistical technique that determines how consumers value different product attributes by presenting product profiles and analyzing stated preferences through regression.
A supply chain strategy in which a product is manufactured to a semi-finished state and held as a generic subassembly; the differentiating step is performed as late as possible in the supply network.
An attribute that is both important to consumers and varies meaningfully across competing products, making it a key driver of actual choice behavior.
A binary (0/1) indicator variable used in OLS regression to represent whether a specific attribute level is present in a given profile. One level per attribute is omitted as the baseline.
A conjoint design in which only a statistically chosen subset of all attribute-level combinations is shown to respondents, reducing survey length while allowing unbiased estimation of main effects.
A conjoint study design in which respondents evaluate every possible combination of attribute levels. The number of profiles equals the product of the number of levels across all attributes.
The specific values or categories that an attribute can take in a conjoint study.
A production strategy combining the efficiency of mass production with individual customization, typically enabled by modular product architecture and postponement.
A product architecture in which functional elements are grouped into self-contained modules that can be independently designed, produced, and swapped with minimal effect on other modules.
The statistical method used in conjoint analysis to estimate part-worth utilities by regressing preference scores on dummy-coded attribute level variables to produce coefficient estimates.
The incremental value in preference units that a consumer places on a specific attribute level, estimated as the OLS regression coefficient on the corresponding dummy variable.
A Japanese fail-proofing design philosophy that builds physical or procedural safeguards into a product or process to prevent user errors before they occur.
The arrangement of functional elements of a product into physical or logical chunks that serve as building blocks for a product or family of products.
A single product concept in a conjoint study, defined by one specific level from each attribute. Respondents evaluate profiles to reveal their underlying preferences.
The percentage contribution of each attribute to total preference variation. Calculated as (attribute utility range / sum of all attribute utility ranges) × 100.
The maximum dollar amount a consumer would pay for an attribute upgrade, derived from conjoint results by scaling an attribute's utility gain by the price sensitivity coefficient.
